question: [File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getOldestObservationSecondsAgo / consult -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX -> _swapTitanXForTorus] Can an unprivileged attacker who flash-loan-manipulates the TitanX/TORUS Uniswap V3 pool spot price in the same block as a newly initialized pool (observationCardinality=1, oldest observation age = seconds) cause getOldestObservationSecondsAgo to return a value of 1-2 seconds, collapsing the TWAP window to near-spot, so that getTorusQuoteForTitanX returns a manipulated quote, setting amountOutMinimum near zero in _swapTitanXForTorus, allowing the attacker to sandwich swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn and extract the protocol's TitanX allocation? Preconditions: pool recently initialized, observationCardinality=1, attacker controls a flash loan. Call sequence: [flash loan large TitanX, swap to move pool price, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn with attacker-controlled deadline, swap back, repay flash loan]. Invariant tested: amountOutMinimum for _swapTitanXForTorus must reflect a fair TWAP price, not a manipulated spot. Scoped impact: attacker extracts TitanX allocated to the burn interval at below-market Torus output, profiting from the sandwich. Local proof idea: fork test on pinned block 25530134, deploy mock pool with cardinality=1, manipulate price, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, assert torusBurned < expected fair-price output by >slippage tolerance.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getOldestObservationSecondsAgo / consult -> getTitanXQuoteForETH -> _swapETHForTitanX -> _distributeFees -> createTorus] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the TITAN_X_ETH_POOL TWAP window by exploiting a short oldest-observation age (getOldestObservationSecondsAgo returns < 15 min), causing getTitanXQuoteForETH to return an inflated TitanX-per-ETH quote, so that getEthAmountForTitanX in _distributeFees returns a deflated ETH cost, allowing the attacker to call createTorus with ETH and pay far less than the fair market ETH cost for a given power/lengthInDays, minting excess TORUS and earning disproportionate staking rewards? Preconditions: TITAN_X_ETH_POOL has few observations (oldest < 900 seconds), attacker can move pool price. Call sequence: [manipulate TITAN_X_ETH_POOL price to make TitanX appear cheap in ETH, call createTorus{value: manipulated_low_ETH}(power, lengthInDays), claim rewards after endTime]. Invariant tested: ETH paid for createTorus must equal or exceed fair-market ETH equivalent of costTitanX. Scoped impact: attacker mints TORUS positions at below-market cost, extracting excess TORUS rewards from the reward pool. Local proof idea: fork test, warp pool to low-cardinality state, manipulate price, call createTorus, assert mintedTorus/ETHpaid ratio exceeds fair-market ratio.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: consult -> arithmeticMeanTick rounding -> getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the systematic negative-infinity rounding of arithmeticMeanTick in consult (line 103: arithmeticMeanTick-- when tickCumulativesDelta < 0 and remainder != 0) to obtain a consistently lower tick than the true TWAP, causing getTorusQuoteForTitanX to underestimate the Torus output, setting amountOutMinimum below fair value in _swapTitanXForTorus, enabling a sandwich attack on every swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn call? Preconditions: pool price trending downward (negative tickCumulativesDelta with non-zero remainder), attacker monitors mempool. Call sequence: [attacker front-runs swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn with a large sell, victim tx executes with low amountOutMinimum, attacker back-runs to restore price]. Invariant tested: amountOutMinimum must be >= fair TWAP output minus allowed slippage; tick rounding must not create a systematic exploitable bias. Scoped impact: attacker extracts value from each burn interval swap via MEV sandwich. Local proof idea: unit test consult with tickCumulativesDelta=-1 mod secondsAgo != 0, verify arithmeticMeanTick is one tick lower than true mean, compute resulting quote difference, assert sandwich profit > gas cost.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 (high-price branch) -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the precision loss in the high-price branch of getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 (when sqrtRatioX96 > type(uint128).max, ratioX128 = Math.mulDiv(sqrtRatioX96, sqrtRatioX96, 1<<64) loses 64 bits of precision) to obtain a systematically lower Torus quote, reducing amountOutMinimum in _swapTitanXForTorus, enabling sandwich extraction on swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn? Preconditions: TitanX/TORUS pool price is in a range where sqrtPriceX96 > type(uint128).max (i.e., Torus is very cheap relative to TitanX). Call sequence: [verify pool sqrtPriceX96 > 2^128, call getTorusQuoteForTitanX, compare to full-precision reference, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, sandwich]. Invariant tested: getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 must return a value within 1 wei of the full-precision result regardless of sqrtRatioX96 magnitude. Scoped impact: systematic underprice of amountOutMinimum enables MEV extraction on every burn swap. Local proof idea: fuzz test getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 with sqrtRatioX96 in range (2^128, 2^160), compare both branches, assert precision loss < 1 bps.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: consult -> harmonicMeanLiquidity -> uint192 overflow boundary] Can an unprivileged attacker trigger an arithmetic overflow in the harmonicMeanLiquidity calculation (line 108: secondsAgoUint192 * uint192(type(uint160).max)) by passing secondsAgo = type(uint32).max (4294967295) to consult, since uint32.max * uint160.max ≈ 6.28e57 which is at the boundary of uint192.max ≈ 6.28e57, causing an overflow revert that blocks all oracle-dependent functions (swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, swapETHForTorusAndBurn, createTorus, stakeTorus)? Preconditions: caller controls secondsAgo parameter passed to consult; getOldestObservationSecondsAgo returns a value >= type(uint32).max (impossible in practice but testable). Call sequence: [call consult(pool, type(uint32).max), observe revert or overflow]. Invariant tested: consult must not overflow for any valid uint32 secondsAgo value. Scoped impact: if reachable, blocks reward extraction paths; if silently overflows, harmonicMeanLiquidity is corrupted. Local proof idea: unit test consult with secondsAgo = type(uint32).max and mock pool returning max secondsPerLiquidityCumulativesDelta, assert no overflow.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getOldestObservationSecondsAgo -> consult -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the case where getOldestObservationSecondsAgo returns 0 (observationTimestamp == block.timestamp, possible when pool was just initialized in the same block) causing secondsAgo to be set to 0, which then causes consult to revert with 'BP' (require(secondsAgo != 0)), blocking swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn and swapETHForTorusAndBurn for the entire block, and if the attacker is the only one who can call these functions in the next interval, gaining exclusive access to the incentive fee? Preconditions: attacker can initialize or interact with the pool in the same block as a burn call. Call sequence: [attacker triggers pool initialization in block N, all burn calls in block N revert, attacker calls burn in block N+1 exclusively]. Invariant tested: oracle fallback must handle secondsAgo=0 without reverting or must use a safe minimum. Scoped impact: attacker monopolizes incentive fee extraction across intervals. Local proof idea: fork test, simulate pool init in same block, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, assert revert, then call in next block and assert attacker receives full incentive.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: consult -> secondsPerLiquidityCumulativesDelta = 0 -> division by zero in harmonicMeanLiquidity] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a division-by-zero revert in consult (line 109: uint192(secondsPerLiquidityCumulativesDelta) << 32 == 0) by interacting with a pool where no liquidity was active during the observation window (secondsPerLiquidityCumulativeX128s[1] == secondsPerLiquidityCumulativeX128s[0]), blocking all oracle-dependent swap and fee functions, and then being the first to call them after the condition clears to capture the accumulated interval allocation? Preconditions: pool has zero in-range liquidity for the entire secondsAgo window. Call sequence: [remove all in-range liquidity from TitanX/TORUS pool, wait for observation window, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, observe revert, re-add liquidity, call again to capture full interval]. Invariant tested: consult must handle zero secondsPerLiquidityCumulativesDelta gracefully or callers must handle the revert. Scoped impact: attacker can time exclusive access to interval incentives. Local proof idea: fork test with mock pool returning equal secondsPerLiquidityCumulative values, assert revert in consult, then assert attacker captures next interval.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getOldestObservationSecondsAgo -> observationCardinality=1 edge case] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the edge case in getOldestObservationSecondsAgo where observationCardinality=1 causes (observationIndex+1) % 1 == 0, always reading observations[0] (the current slot), so observationTimestamp is always the most recent timestamp, making secondsAgo = block.timestamp - recentTimestamp = very small, collapsing the TWAP to near-spot for getTorusQuoteForTitanX and getTitanXQuoteForETH? Preconditions: TitanX/TORUS pool or TITAN_X_ETH_POOL has observationCardinality=1 (never increased beyond initial). Call sequence: [verify pool cardinality=1, manipulate spot price, call getTorusQuoteForTitanX, observe near-spot quote, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn with sandwich]. Invariant tested: oracle must use a minimum observation window regardless of cardinality; cardinality=1 must not collapse TWAP to spot. Local proof idea: fork test with pool at cardinality=1, manipulate price 10x, call getTorusQuoteForTitanX, assert quote differs from fair TWAP by >5%.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getTitanXQuoteForETH -> _swapETHForTitanX -> _distributeFees -> createTorus (ETH path)] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the TITAN_X_ETH_POOL TWAP to make ETH appear more valuable (TitanX cheaper per ETH), causing getTitanXQuoteForETH to return a high TitanX amount for a given ETH input, which then causes getEthAmountForTitanX (inverse lookup) to return a low ETH requirement, so that _distributeFees accepts a small msg.value as sufficient payment for a large power createTorus, minting excess TORUS and claiming disproportionate rewards? Preconditions: attacker can move TITAN_X_ETH_POOL price (large TitanX holder or flash loan), oldest observation < 15 min. Call sequence: [buy large TitanX with ETH to inflate TitanX price in ETH terms, call createTorus{value: small_ETH}(maxPower, maxDays), sell TitanX back, wait for endTime, claim rewards]. Invariant tested: ETH paid in createTorus must be >= fair-market ETH value of costTitanX at time of creation. Scoped impact: attacker mints maximum TORUS at minimum ETH cost, extracting excess rewards from the daily reward pool. Local proof idea: fork test, flash-loan manipulate TITAN_X_ETH_POOL, call createTorus with 10% of fair ETH, assert mintedTorus/ETHpaid > 10x fair ratio.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getTorusQuoteForTitanX -> _swapTitanXForTorus -> swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the TitanX/TORUS pool TWAP to return a low Torus quote, causing _swapTitanXForTorus in swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild to set a very low amountOutMinimum, allowing the attacker to sandwich the swap and extract TitanX from the build interval allocation, while still receiving the 1.5% INCENTIVE_FEE_BUILD incentive? Preconditions: attacker monitors mempool for swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild calls, pool has short observation window. Call sequence: [front-run: sell Torus for TitanX to depress Torus price, victim swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild executes with low amountOutMinimum, back-run: buy Torus back]. Invariant tested: amountOutMinimum in _swapTitanXForTorus must protect against sandwich attacks regardless of oracle window length. Scoped impact: attacker extracts value from build interval TitanX allocation via MEV. Local proof idea: fork test, simulate sandwich around swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild, measure Torus received vs fair TWAP output, assert sandwich profit > 0.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: consult -> int24 truncation of arithmeticMeanTick] Can an unprivileged attacker trigger a silent int24 truncation in consult (line 98: int24(tickCumulativesDelta / secondsAgoInt56)) when tickCumulativesDelta / secondsAgo produces a value outside int24 range (-8388608 to 8388607), causing arithmeticMeanTick to wrap to an incorrect value, producing a wildly incorrect sqrtPriceX96 from TickMath.getSqrtRatioAtTick, and thus a manipulated quote that enables reward extraction? Preconditions: pool tick is near the int24 boundary (tick > 887272 or < -887272 is impossible in Uniswap V3, but cumulative division could produce boundary values with extreme secondsAgo). Call sequence: [set up pool at extreme tick, call consult with large secondsAgo, observe truncated arithmeticMeanTick, call getTorusQuoteForTitanX, compare to expected]. Invariant tested: arithmeticMeanTick must always be within valid Uniswap V3 tick range after int24 cast; no silent truncation. Local proof idea: fuzz test consult with tickCumulatives near int24 boundary values, assert arithmeticMeanTick == expected without truncation.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 -> token ordering branch (baseToken < quoteToken)] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the token address ordering in getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 (line 159: baseToken < quoteToken determines mulDiv direction) to obtain an incorrect quote when TITAN_X_ADDRESS and torusToken address ordering does not match the pool's token0/token1 ordering, causing getTorusQuoteForTitanX to return an inverted or incorrect price, setting amountOutMinimum to near-zero or near-infinity in _swapTitanXForTorus? Preconditions: TITAN_X_ADDRESS > address(torusToken) (Torus is token0), which changes the mulDiv branch. Call sequence: [verify token ordering: TITAN_X_ADDRESS vs torusToken address, call getTorusQuoteForTitanX with large baseAmount, compare to pool spot price, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn]. Invariant tested: getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 must return the correct directional quote matching the pool's actual token0/token1 assignment. Scoped impact: if inverted, amountOutMinimum is near-zero, enabling full sandwich extraction of burn interval. Local proof idea: unit test getQuoteForSqrtRatioX96 with both token orderings against known pool sqrtPriceX96, assert quote matches pool spot within 1 bps.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getOldestObservationSecondsAgo -> uninitialized observation slot] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the uninitialized observation slot handling in getOldestObservationSecondsAgo (lines 136-138: if !initialized, fall back to observations[0]) to cause the function to return a stale or incorrect secondsAgo when the pool's observation array is in the process of expanding cardinality, leading to a collapsed TWAP window and enabling price manipulation for reward extraction? Preconditions: pool observationCardinality is being increased (increaseObservationCardinalityNext called but not yet filled), next slot is uninitialized. Call sequence: [trigger cardinality increase on TitanX/TORUS pool, immediately call getTorusQuoteForTitanX, observe secondsAgo = block.timestamp - observations[0].timestamp (potentially very small), manipulate price, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn]. Invariant tested: oracle must use a safe minimum window even during cardinality expansion. Scoped impact: attacker exploits cardinality expansion window to collapse TWAP and sandwich burn swaps. Local proof idea: fork test, call increaseObservationCardinalityNext on pool, immediately call getTorusQuoteForTitanX, assert secondsAgo >= minimum safe window.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getTitanXQuoteForETH -> _swapETHForTitanX (in TorusBuyAndProcess) -> swapETHForTorusAndBurn] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the TITAN_X_ETH_POOL TWAP to return a low TitanX-per-ETH quote, causing _swapETHForTitanX in swapETHForTorusAndBurn to set a very low adjustedTitanXAmount (amountOutMinimum), allowing the attacker to sandwich the ETH->TitanX swap and extract ETH from the burn interval allocation, while still receiving the 1.5% ETH incentive? Invariant tested: adjustedTitanXAmount must protect against sandwich attacks on the ETH->TitanX leg. Scoped impact: attacker extracts ETH from burn interval via MEV on the ETH->TitanX swap. [File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getEthAmountForTitanX (TorusCreateAndStake) -> _distributeFees -> stakeTorus] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the TITAN_X_ETH_POOL TWAP to reduce the ETH cost returned by getEthAmountForTitanX, allowing them to call stakeTorus with a small msg.value that passes the requiredETH check, paying below-market fees, and then claim disproportionate staking rewards from the reward pool? Call sequence: [manipulate TITAN_X_ETH_POOL to make TitanX appear cheap in ETH, call stakeTorus{value: manipulated_low_ETH}(largeTorusAmount, maxDays), restore price, wait for endTime, claim]. Invariant tested: ETH paid in stakeTorus must equal fair-market ETH equivalent of the 5% TitanX fee. Scoped impact: attacker stakes at below-market cost, extracting excess TORUS rewards from the daily reward pool. [File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: consult -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX -> _swapTitanXForTorus -> swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn (double amountBurned write)] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the double-write of currInterval.amountBurned in swapETHForTorusAndBurn (lines 270 and 275 both set currInterval.amountBurned = currInterval.amountAllocated) combined with a manipulated oracle to cause accounting inconsistency, where the interval is marked as burned before the oracle-dependent swap executes, and if the swap reverts due to oracle manipulation, the interval is permanently locked with amountBurned set but no actual burn occurring? Preconditions: attacker can cause _swapETHForTitanX or _swapTitanXForTorus to revert (e.g., by manipulating oracle to return 0 amountOutMinimum that still fails). Call sequence: [manipulate oracle, call swapETHForTorusAndBurn, observe revert after amountBurned is set, verify interval is permanently locked]. Invariant tested: if swap reverts, currInterval.amountBurned must not be set; interval must remain claimable. Scoped impact: attacker permanently locks burn intervals, preventing future burns and accumulating unclaimed allocations. Local proof idea: fork test, manipulate oracle to cause swap revert, assert currInterval.amountBurned == 0 after revert.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getTorusQuoteForTitanX -> adjustedTorusAmount rounding -> _swapTitanXForTorus] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit integer division truncation in adjustedTorusAmount = (expectedTorusAmount * (100 - titanXToTorusSlippage)) / 100 when expectedTorusAmount is very small (oracle returns near-zero quote due to extreme price), causing adjustedTorusAmount = 0, which sets amountOutMinimum = 0 in the Uniswap swap, allowing the attacker to sandwich the swap and receive all TitanX value with zero Torus output? Preconditions: oracle returns a very small Torus quote (e.g., 99 wei), titanXToTorusSlippage = 10, so (99 * 90) / 100 = 89, but if quote < 100/90 = 1, adjustedTorusAmount = 0. Call sequence: [manipulate TitanX/TORUS pool to extreme price, call getTorusQuoteForTitanX, verify adjustedTorusAmount = 0, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, sandwich to extract all TitanX]. Invariant tested: adjustedTorusAmount must never be 0 when amountTitanX > 0; a minimum output floor must exist. Scoped impact: attacker extracts full TitanX burn allocation with zero Torus burned. Local proof idea: unit test _swapTitanXForTorus with mock oracle returning 1 wei quote, assert amountOutMinimum > 0 or revert.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getTitanXQuoteForETH -> adjustedTitanXAmount rounding -> _swapETHForTitanX] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit integer division truncation in adjustedTitanXAmount = (expectedTitanXAmount * (100 - ethToTitanXSlippage)) / 100 when getTitanXQuoteForETH returns a near-zero value (oracle manipulated to show ETH is nearly worthless in TitanX terms), causing adjustedTitanXAmount = 0 and amountOutMinimum = 0 in the ETH->TitanX swap, enabling a sandwich attack on swapETHForTorusAndBurn? Preconditions: TITAN_X_ETH_POOL manipulated to extreme price, oldest observation < 15 min. Call sequence: [manipulate TITAN_X_ETH_POOL to make ETH appear worthless, call swapETHForTorusAndBurn, sandwich ETH->TitanX swap with amountOutMinimum=0]. Invariant tested: adjustedTitanXAmount must never be 0 when ethAmount > 0. Scoped impact: attacker extracts ETH from burn interval with near-zero TitanX output. Local proof idea: unit test _swapETHForTitanX with mock oracle returning 1 wei TitanX quote, assert amountOutMinimum > 0.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: consult -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX -> _swapTitanXForTorus -> _addLiquidityBuild -> swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the TitanX/TORUS TWAP to cause _swapTitanXForTorus in swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild to receive far fewer Torus tokens than expected (due to low amountOutMinimum), then pass this small Torus amount to _addLiquidityBuild, which uses liquiditySlippage-based minimums, causing the liquidity addition to fail or add negligible liquidity while the attacker captures the TitanX incentive fee? Preconditions: oracle manipulated, liquiditySlippage = 10%, attacker calls swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild. Call sequence: [manipulate oracle, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild, observe small torusPurchased, observe _addLiquidityBuild with mismatched amounts, attacker receives INCENTIVE_FEE_BUILD]. Invariant tested: liquidity added in _addLiquidityBuild must be proportional to the TitanX allocated; oracle manipulation must not allow incentive extraction with negligible liquidity contribution. Scoped impact: attacker extracts 1.5% TitanX incentive while contributing near-zero liquidity to the protocol. Local proof idea: fork test, manipulate oracle, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild, assert liquidityAdded > 0 and proportional to allocation.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getOldestObservationSecondsAgo -> consult -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX (cross-block TWAP manipulation)] Can an unprivileged attacker with a large TitanX position gradually shift the TitanX/TORUS pool TWAP over multiple blocks (not a single-block flash loan) to persistently depress the Torus quote returned by getTorusQuoteForTitanX, causing all subsequent swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn calls to have a low amountOutMinimum, enabling repeated sandwich attacks across multiple intervals? Preconditions: attacker holds enough TitanX to move pool price over 15 minutes, pool has 15-min TWAP window. Call sequence: [over 15+ minutes, gradually sell Torus for TitanX to depress Torus price, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn in each interval with sandwich]. Invariant tested: TWAP oracle must resist gradual multi-block manipulation; amountOutMinimum must reflect fair long-term price. Scoped impact: attacker extracts value from multiple consecutive burn intervals. Local proof idea: fork test, simulate 15-min price manipulation via multiple blocks, call getTorusQuoteForTitanX, assert quote reflects manipulated TWAP, measure sandwich profit per interval.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getEthAmountForTitanX -> _distributeFees -> createTorus (ETH path) -> reward pool inflation] Can an unprivileged attacker use oracle manipulation to underpay ETH fees in createTorus, causing _distributeFees to send less ETH to distributeETHForBurning and distributeETHForBuilding than the fair market amount, thereby reducing the burn/build pool balances and future interval allocations, while the attacker's own TORUS position earns rewards from a now-smaller pool (but at a proportionally larger share due to lower cost)? Call sequence: [manipulate oracle, call createTorus{value: low_ETH}(maxPower, maxDays), observe reduced ETH sent to burn/build pools, claim rewards]. Invariant tested: total ETH distributed to burn/build pools must equal fair-market ETH equivalent of fees collected. Scoped impact: attacker gains excess reward share while depleting protocol burn/build reserves. Local proof idea: fork test, compare totalETHBurn before/after manipulated createTorus vs fair-price createTorus, assert deficit.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: consult -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX -> burnFees (indirect)] Can an unprivileged attacker call burnFees() (which collects LP fees and calls burnTorus) at a time when the oracle-derived Torus balance in the contract is non-zero due to a prior manipulated swap leaving dust Torus, causing burnTorus to burn more Torus than intended, deflating supply and benefiting the attacker's existing TORUS holdings? Preconditions: attacker holds TORUS, prior swap left Torus dust in contract. Call sequence: [manipulate oracle to cause _swapTitanXForTorus to receive more Torus than expected (oracle overestimates), call burnFees, observe burnTorus burns extra Torus, attacker's TORUS appreciates]. Invariant tested: burnTorus must only burn Torus that was legitimately acquired through protocol swaps, not oracle-manipulation artifacts. Scoped impact: attacker inflates value of held TORUS by burning excess protocol Torus. Local proof idea: fork test, manipulate oracle to cause excess Torus in contract, call burnFees, assert torusBurned == expected protocol amount.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: getOldestObservationSecondsAgo -> consult (TITAN_X_ETH_POOL) -> getEthAmountForTitanX -> _distributeFees -> _swapETHForTitanX (fee portion)] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the TITAN_X_ETH_POOL oracle to cause _swapETHForTitanX (called within _distributeFees for the titanXBurnFee portion) to receive fewer TitanX tokens than expected (low amountOutMinimum), allowing a sandwich attack on this internal swap, extracting ETH that was meant to be converted to TitanX and burned? Preconditions: attacker monitors createTorus/stakeTorus calls, TITAN_X_ETH_POOL has short observation window. Call sequence: [front-run createTorus: manipulate TITAN_X_ETH_POOL, createTorus executes _swapETHForTitanX with low amountOutMinimum, back-run to restore price]. Invariant tested: TitanX received from fee ETH swap must be >= fair-market amount; sandwich must not extract fee ETH. Scoped impact: attacker extracts ETH from the titanXBurnFee portion of every createTorus/stakeTorus call. Local proof idea: fork test, sandwich _swapETHForTitanX in _distributeFees, measure TitanX received vs fair amount.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/library/OracleLibrary.sol -> Scope: Critical reward extraction or unfair reward access by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: consult -> getTorusQuoteForTitanX -> swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn -> releaseFractalFunds (triggered inside)] Can an unprivileged attacker time a call to swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn exactly when block.timestamp >= lastFractalRelease + FRACTAL_RELEASE_INTERVAL, causing releaseFractalFunds to be triggered inside the burn function, which moves fractalTitanX into totalTitanXBurn, and then exploit the oracle to sandwich the subsequent burn swap that now has a larger allocation, extracting more value than a normal interval? Preconditions: block.timestamp >= lastFractalRelease + 110 days, fractalTitanX > 0, oracle manipulable. Call sequence: [wait for fractal release window, manipulate oracle, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, observe releaseFractalFunds triggered, sandwich the larger allocation swap]. Invariant tested: fractal fund release must not create a larger-than-expected single-interval allocation that can be sandwiched.
